module: Module 1 |Introduction to Sustainability Matters in SMEs
content: In this interview, I'm joined by Ishaan Ajay. Ishaan is program manager for a multimillion dollar CSR initiative focused on local entrepreneurship. Now, we'll see why he chooses to use the word local entrepreneurship and not SMEs. Ishaan a very warm welcome for this interview. Thank you so much, ma'am. Ishaan you've started off you know with a very interesting I, I may say growth trajectory. You did your Masters in Business Administration from IIM Indore where you had been exposed to both Indian and international businesses, how they evolved. And you can give us some, understanding about how you got into understanding what small and medium-sized enterprises means and what you learned in the MBA classroom that struck a chord with you. So, your earliest reflections of how you were introduced to the word MSEs Ishaan. Sure ma'am, and thank you so much for having me here. I think it's always been a great experience whenever we have engaged on these themes in the past as well. About my journey studying the MSME space. I think most colleges these days have local chapters of enactors, of Isaac. When we engaged with local social entrepreneurship ideas that's usually for any student. The first time they hear about MSMEs. Of course, they may not know necessarily what MSME means but the fact that we are working on ideas on business models where you can benefit a particular stakeholder, be it the rural women, be it the urban youth, be it a new technology area as well. It's a very interesting space and I got exposure to that in my first year at IIM Indore during the undergraduate program. Thankfully, the IIM Indore program was also a program focused around building socially responsible leaders. So, in courses regarding sociology regarding business governance and society which the recently retired director, Professor Rishi used to teach us we explored multiple facets of what it means to build a real business. That is also socially responsible but is financially sustainable. That was the starting point, I think, and it's been, I think 11, 12 years when that happened. And since then, it's been a really interesting journey because I have had a chance to see all kinds of businesses in the small and medium enterprise space. And I'll use the term local entrepreneurship rather than SME because there are cooperatives, there are NGOs, there are social enterprises, and there are fully for profit businesses, and then there are startups. All of them can be part of the SME space. And thankfully, I think fortunately I've had a chance to engage with all of them at different points in life, and that's what's kept me so motivated about studying this space continuously, and I continue to do so even today. That's wonderful to know Ishaan and to our students who are listening to this interview, I'm sure the first few sentences that Ishaan mentioned has you know, taken your thoughts into different directions because there are so many aspects of facets to the word MSEs. And as Ishaan rightly mentioned, it is a sector that is so vast and diverse and it can be used in different types of words or different types of vocabulary, depending on the context and depending on the country. And one very essential aspect that Ishaan has also had in his career path is to have a fellowship with member of Parliament in India. And that engagement, I think gave him a wonderful exposure to further leverage that on this interest that he had. So, Ishaan I want you to tell us about the program the fellowship and what you learned from the grassroots level. Sure, ma'am. So it's called the LAMP Fellowship. It's legislative assistant to a member of parliament. There's a proper training curriculum that students who are interested in this fellowship undergo for a month, and there's an age limit of 25 years or younger. We are all mapped to around 50 members of parliament across party lines without any say in who we want to work with. It's all randomly assigned, and our mandate is that we can only engage in nonpolitical work. Right. How that affected my particular learnings was that I got a really good opportunity to engage with the MPs social and public commitments around local entrepreneurship. I got a chance to engage with his constituents who had their businesses. I got a chance to meet with different stakeholders. So, was part the MP, I was working with was part of the CII, Confederation of Indian Industries Parliamentary Forum. And there we used to have a lot of conversations around. Building the local entrepreneurship space further providing more support to MSEs. And then we also engaged deeply on policy matters around this because incidentally, when I was doing that fellowship, COVID hit and the MSEs were the most impacted of all business groups in India. Very true. And so we worked really hard to study what were the support initiatives being provided by different governments across the world? How the private sector was coming together? How the NGO space was coming together? What kind of technology adoption was required to facilitate a lot of the interventions? And how business models actually transformed? The world was not the same after COVID. Right. And in that sense, even for MSEs, the implications were really, really meaningful and transformative in many ways. And thankfully I got a chance to witness that firsthand. I continued with the member of Parliament for two years after the fellowship was over and continued to build on all of that knowledge and resource. Advocating for a lot of changes in the Parliament itself via the MPF course. That was a very interesting time for me, and I think it's a worthy experience for anyone interested in studying policymaking at the grassroots level in India. Yeah, that's wonderful Ishaan, because you not only then understand the space and the sector but you also understand what is the government doing. To create that ecosystem. And that is what many entrepreneurs struggle to find out. You know, they, they get snatches of information from here and there. They may get it on government websites but you actually saw it from the policy level. And you also saw that in a democracy, how parliament questions as well as response to such queries. And I think that must have been a very immersive experience. It indeed was ma'am. And I can share, I have a personal compilation of my own of around 26 to 27 schemes thanks to all of this exposure that the government has released that people never get to hear about on a daily basis that benefit different facets of setting up a MSME unit. There are specific grants or support schemes for women-owned businesses, for businesses that belong to people from disadvantaged communities. There are grants, rebates and support mechanisms for marketing expenditure, for setting up a warehouse, for doing your quality testing, for ensuring that you have better e-commerce linkages. So, to be able to study all of that, I think I was really fortunate. I got to see what kind of letters or appeals different members make right to start these initiatives? There was so much clamour in the tourism and hospitality sector at that time because of the lockdown, and they were all writing to us and we were raising those issues with the government with the ministers. Then those responses would come in the parliament, and my job would be to track every single response that came in to see is there a new initiative being announced? Is there something we have missed communicating to our constituents? Again, I think the learnings you get from these kind of experiences are not usually seen in the public eye, but they're really, really valuable when you look at, look back at your experiences and how you contextualize things. Very true. And for our learners, I want you to reflect upon what Ishaan said just now because very often the information comes to you through the media or through schemes which are most successful, or which you know, certain sections of the society or certain ministers choose to make public. But as Ishaan said, there are a whole number of much more interesting schemes, which are like women focused or for people who are specially abled or for region specific. And so therefore, the questions that obviously came into the parliament would have been from parliamentarians whose regions had those. That's correct. You know, issues whose regions were specific to those kind of entrepreneurs. For instance, fishing or for instance you know, that is very specific to maybe the Himalaya Mountains or the Western Ghats. Like I mentioned, like I've mentioned in in my book, that you will have with your media and climate change making sense of press narratives the, the region specific problems of people also have to be taken into account and therefore the policy will be directed towards those aspects of it. Because at the end of the day you know, it is the people that matter during the elections and in a democracy, if the people are not happy with the schemes that the government is giving out, then it al also affects the way in which power is kind of distributed in that region. In fact, a very. Interesting example of this is the support provided to the Northeastern states, okay, and the Hill states. Usually you'll see across all schemes, there'll be like a small 10 to 15% additional benefit that these states can get because of the historically weak linkages. Because of the historical disadvantage in connectivity, in logistics. And now suddenly you see the northeast, for example, becoming a hub of fisheries, a hub of animal husbandry, a hub of non-timber forest produce, and that's also directly linked to the schemes for tribal communities. And it's not just the micro, small and medium enterprise ministry, the MSME Ministry, you have the Ministry of Skill Development with its MSME initiatives. You have the Ministry of Textiles with their MSME initiatives, the tribal welfare, social justice, agriculture ministries. All of them are doing something or the other. And it's really worth going into their websites, looking at what are the announcements in the recent times and what is the intent behind which they come, what gap in the policy space are they trying to address? Because for students who are in the first year of undergrad they may not understand the nuances of these schemes yet but just building that base of the fact that, yes, the spectrum is really vast, allows them to understand that this is not a one-time initiative. It's a whole ecosystem that's being built by the government and not just the Indian government. Different state governments and governments abroad as well are all trying to do the same thing. This is where policy making around MSEs is headed now. A whole enabling ecosystem, touching the entire spectrum of activities and sectors in a particular region or social area. That's interesting Ishaan and as you were talking I was also wondering if you could give us examples of foreign countries or foreign nations that are interested in investing in specific SMEs in, in rural sectors like handloom or you know specific industries that kind of, or the youth led initiatives. So, if you, there's anything that you can recollect so that our young students can also kind of understand how to think about those avenues. Certainly ma'am. I think. Thankfully I've had a chance to travel across a few countries. Some of the most interesting social development across the SME space kind of initiatives come from the Latin American region and Africa. You'll see initiatives, and I'll give you a very small example. There was a startup now MSEs. Yeah, are classified as such, just on the basis of their size. They could be a startup. They could just be a mom and pop store. Correct. They could be a local kirana store. So, this was a startup. It was a tech startup and it only provided one service. It helped restaurants and hotels that were of a smaller scale could not invest on their own digital platforms for human resource management or customer management. They were helping just digitize those processes by one time service. And that was an SME. That had benefited very strongly by the government's initiatives on tech and digital adoption. This was an SME, I think based of North Africa, prevalent across Egypt neighboring countries, mostly Egypt, Sudan, and Ethiopia was the kind of space that it was following. I'm blanking on the name right now but a very, very interesting example of an SME that was leveraging government grants to provide ech-adoption across the hospitality sector and help in the digitization process, which is essentially a, a must have these days for most vibrant industries. In the Latin American region, you see a lot of blended finance models that are being supported by the government. That also involves a lot of time, big cooperates. Because usually if you look at companies like Nestle, their plantations for coffee Yeah, are in Latin America, in Bolivia, in Peru. And so there were a lot of schemes where the government and Nestle were partnering to create blended finance instruments that allowed these SMEs to have grants for, for example, boosting the quality of the ecosystem around the coffee plantation so that the productivity increased, making them more climate resilient or even helping with skill building initiatives at a large level. And in fact, the skill development sector is the recipient of the most amount of government aid because the gap is in the skilled labour versus the unskilled labour for SMEs today. So, all the schemes that you want to. Check in terms of their impact implementation. I think the skill sector is a very interesting case. Studies that people can research on their own. Very nice. And I think the local kind of governments is also something that you need to tap into the local panchayat or the district, like you, you talked about the skill ecosystem, so there's a district skill committee. It's, important to also see what your district is choosing as that one district, one product. What are the schemes that they deem to seem fit to subsidize or promote? And I think it's good to link that so that when you become an entrepreneur and, and you want to do something very highly focused or localized, then you must tap into the local ecosystem as well. I'll give you an example of what my company is doing right now. We recently signed a a memorandum of understanding, essentially an in a non-financial agreement with the government of Madhya Pradesh. I was at an event last week where the government of Madhya Pradesh was there, and they had representatives of this Chanderi Sari cluster. Chanderi is a place in Madhya Pradesh, famous for a particular type of sarees. And those people were there. These were all you wouldn't call them the urban elite or the urban educated people, could barely speak English. But had really, really interesting and vibrant products. They could really use a big boost in market linkages, in terms of incomes, in terms of improvement in the quality of outcomes, what our job is and we tied up for that, but exact purpose with the Madhya Pradesh government. We provide capacity building support, skilling support to the Chanderi saree cluster MSEs. We help them get understanding of what it means to be online, on the digital e-commerce space. What are the requirements? How to make sure that your business can withstand the risks of an e-commerce business, because no new market comes without its own risks, right. And so, we partnered with the Madhya Pradesh government as a private entity to jointly support and capacity build about a 100-200 small enterprises focused on making s for the women across the country. And I think it's a great example of how the government and the private sector comes together to support these kind of initiatives at the localist of the levels, the urban, rural, and urban intersection areas, which are not very well skilled, not very well educated in the western sense of the word but extremely enterprising, extremely resilient, and with the right opportunities. They have a very bright future ahead of them. Right. So, going back to your you know, your learning in, in this field, I think we, we also discussed you know, how you got so engaged into the grassroots ecosystem but then you also decided to kind of move on and pursue a masters. You wanted to get back into learning. So, what you know, what was the reason for you to kind of think about pursuing a Masters and that too in studying environmental policy and sustainable finance themes at the Fletcher School of Policy? So, my main motivation was that I realized that there's still a lot about the innovation in the space, the technicalities of how to finance SMEs, for example, that I did not understand very well. You can do your MBA, but in India and being fully conscious of the fact that I'm sitting in the premier business school of the country and I also belong to, as an alum to one of the top schools the indoor campus. But I think I felt that we never studied this level of detail around what is happening across the world in the space. For one, I never had a class in the MBA years that was focused specifically on building sustainability for SMEs and which is happening now at IIM Bangalore. And I realized that sustainability can mean so many different things for people. Sustainability can be about making your business more resilient to shocks. That's a risk management function but it can also mean contributing to lowering the carbon emissions in the environment. So, that's more of an impact facing dimension. And the level of granularity that you can get into around the space because companies are not of one kind. And so there is no solution that is a one size fits all. Right. You have to really go into the weeds of how different people are doing it. When I applied to study to the Fletcher School, at that time, climate finance or impact investing or sustainability finance were not terms that I had been acquainted with before. I had never heard of those conversations happening in India. I'm sure they were happening but if someone sitting at IIM Indore and working with a member of Parliament did not hear about it. I'm pretty sure a vast majority of the country was not yet aware that these are conversations happening, and I wanted to be aware these conversations were being led, were being decided. The Dean of the Fletcher School at that time, she's still one of the foremost experts on climate finance. The professor who taught me finance, he helped set up sovereign investment vehicles for a lot of governments. So, I thought I wanted to go to a place where those conversations are being initiated and being led, and then come back to India and see how those learnings then translate to what kind of work is being happening in the Indian market. There was another reason where I wanted to sort of upscale myself and that was. India requires, or not just India, the developing world, the global South requires contextualization but essentially adoption of the exact same policies and initiatives that are happening across the world. So that convenience space of being an Indian who has studied the SME ecosystem in India and has studied what the Western countries are doing in their respective geographies, and then trying to adapt. Modify and contextualize those to help people in India was a very strong motivation for me to go to the Fletcher School. It was a very diverse and international school. And I spent the next two years studying there just trying to absorb as much as I could from as many different experts that I met, and I always made reflection notes on how they can tie back to the work being done in India. That's wonderful. Alicia and I, I think your heart was firmly in India and that's why you chose to return back and, and use the global perspectives that you had gained there to give back to the society in a meaningful way. We will just pause this interview for some time because I want all my learners of this course to also listen to a podcast that Ishaan had recorded with me while he was a student at Fletcher School and because that podcast also talks about the book that you have as part of this course. So, I would want you to as Ishaan kept referring to reflection notes, I would want you to reflect upon this interview that we've had with Ishaan for these for this duration, as well as listen to the podcast and come up with you know discussion forum comments so that we can see if you have had an, a very engaging session. Thank you so much for listening in. Hello learners and welcome back to our interview series segment with me is Ishaan Ajay. Ishaan is a program manager for a multimillion dollar CSR initiative focused around local entrepreneurship. In our previous segment, you've heard Ishaan talk about his journey from an MBA to doing a master's in a foreign university, coming back to India and getting involved or getting enmeshed in the SMEs ecosystem. In this segment of the interview with Ishaan, we talk about sector specific SMEs, sustainability issues. Welcome back Ishaan. Thank you so much Madam. Ishaan what are some sector specific decarbonization aspects that you want to discuss about that can really help our learners to understand what are the problems that ail especially in today's context where decarbonization is a priority? Sure, ma'am. And I'm glad to be back again for this session. I think it'll be interesting for us to, restrict our discussion this time for deeper dive into the apparel fashion and the technology sort of intersection where technology plays an enabling role in decarbonization. But we talk about the apparel and the fashion technology sector, right. Essentially, and the reason why I find that really interesting is because India is a manufacturing hub of textiles. It's historically always been a manufacturing hub of textiles and the  textiles & apparel industry is alongside oil and gas and animal husbandry, one of the most polluting industries in the world. People who are from the northern part of the country have heard countless stories about the pollution in the river Yamuna and the rivers Ganga and tributaries that are along the way. Specifically in Delhi where I've been living for the last few months we talk about the Yamuna pollution a lot, and most of it is actually the untreated waste from your apparel and die industries all the way from Rohtak to Delhi. So I thought we'll focus on that. Sure  Ishaan. And I think it's also one of the country's most highly populated corridor, the NCR region. We always read about the smog that engulfs the region in the Diwali time. But that's just one aspect of it. Pollution can continue throughout the year and it can also have implications on the health of the population in that region. Definitely and I think in Bangalore here, we've spoken about the Bellandur Lake pollution in such detail. It's made national headlines, the froth and the foam on these lakes. So it's very important to understand where it's coming from and what is being done to, you know this obviously the natural clean of sea waste treatment plants and all, but what is being done at the source of that pollution, and that's where the apparel industry is very critical. Yes and so you were telling us earlier when we were having behind the camera conversation about the fast fashion industry and the waste that is generated through the industry. Textile industry is also one of the highest labor intensive industries in the country. And it focuses more on you know, kind of also exporting textiles to different regions in the world. So wouldn't this have an impact on the industry. If we are talking about sustainability and trying to kind of decarbonize the sector as such, wouldn't it have a larger impact? We I think the parallel can be drawn with technological disruptions. With AI, people said that, oh, people will lose jobs. People aren't losing jobs. The jobs are changing in nature and you constantly have to reinvent and reskill yourself to make sure you keep up with the times. And if you do that, not only do you retain your job, you probably move into a much more qualitatively beneficial job and materially beneficial job. Same with the sector. It's a labor intensive sector. Especially in India automation is at a very low level. It's also a very polluting sector. That has implications, not just for employee welfare and health. It also has implications for their economics, the money that they make. For context, I recently visited an SME that is making vegan leather bags. Vegan leather bags, wallets straps ID planks, accessories. A lot of accessories. Big, small both. I was amazed by that innovation, but at the same time, when I went to the place where they were manufacturing I really wanted to study the implications of the fact that they're using that kind of leather for the employees themselves. And, it's hard to quantify these things in, you know, how much emission has been saved, how much lives have increased. But the fact is that the leather industry again, because a lot of it is part of the apparel, and textile as well with the leather clothes and all contributes so much to the pollution and it contributes so much to the health, adverse health effects of the people who are working in those tanneries. And of course the animal rights dimension of it, you know, with the fact that so many animals are being killed for the leather. There are multiple facets to it. And when we say that what if sustainability decarbonization measures lead to a disruption in the industry, it's not a bad thing at all. If you take the entire spectrum into account, yes, it's capital intensive. A lot of times it requires a major disruption in your business model. But would you not want to have employees who are healthier and have an impact that is a lot more sustainable for you, yourself than what you're doing right now. The conditions are dingy, the health conditions are bad. Bad lighting, bad work environment, congested spaces, all of that needs to be addressed for us to truly be able to decarbonize the industry, the kind of materials we are using, how much of the chemical is being put? And one thing that the apparel industry is particularly guilty of we don't realize a term called microplastics, right. Microplastics come from when you're talking about washing your hands or washing your clothes, the detergents, you can't see them. They're so small. The largest amount of plastic pollution contribution is microplastics going from our industries to our rivers all the way till the oceans. And there's this whole plastic sort of layer in the middle of the Pacific Ocean spanning, lots of kilometers that's just accumulated plastic from the apparel industry and the microplastic pollution. All of that needs to be addressed. And even as we speak Ishaan at Geneva, there are, there is this intergovernmental panel that is on a, with 172 countries trying to come to a conclusion or a consensus on how to deal with plastic. Yes, that treaty has been in the works for a very long time and this is an example of the difficulty that the treaty still hasn't been concluded, and a separate treaty that is also in final stages of, you know, negotiation, but still has been going on for so many years is the High Seas Treaty. And the High Seas Treaty also talks a little bit on the responsibility, the attribution of these pollutive elements. Again, it ties back to the apparel sector in so many ways, and that's why we are talking about the sector today. Right and Ishaan, if you can simplify the word decarbonization for our learners. And in the most simplest terms, how would you define decarbonization and put it in perspective or put it in context for SMEs? Sure, decarbonization, when we talk about it we understand that every single manufacturing activity has its own carbon emissions. And I mentioned that apparel sector, the fashion and textile sector cumulatively has as high emissions as animal has been re and oil and gas, right. Everyone thinks of oil and gas as a major culprit. Fossil fuels as a major culprit, but people don't realize apparel is as big as culprit. So decarbonization would be the process of reducing the carbon intensity, the, the amount of pollution that is being generated by the industry to a level that is sustainable, that aligns with our targets to prevent global warming, and also allows for more long-term, durable sort of industry practices to come on board. When I contextualize it for the SME space. I will say this, this is my personal belief. I don't believe that decarbonization as an initiative refers to any one company, right. You can't decarbonize a company. You can only decarbonize a sector because if I, as a company shut down because of my carbon emissions, someone else will start doing it. That's not a long-term efficient solution. You want a system in place that allows irrespective of the player in the market for you to have lower carbon emissions. And so for the SME space, and I'll refer back to the statistic I gave earlier, 98 to 99% of all registered companies in India are micro, not even small micro enterprises, right. And so, you can think of it all your innovators that will provide a solution to the decarbonization challenge of the apparel industry are mostly micro, small and medium enterprises. And these includes manufacturing companies. Your standard setting companies, rating agencies, verification agencies, technology adopters, technology innovators and sometimes they're different, sometimes they're the same, consulting companies, and then also social sector companies that provide the essential capacity building and skilling support. We can very easily say, do this or do ai, do digitization, but for someone making saree's or you know, sports clothes in a Jalandhar factory or Thrissur factory. They may not understand these things, right? Who is to train them? Who is to tell them what's the significance of all of it? So it's a whole ecosystem, and all of them are mostly SMEs, right? So it's not, you have service sector SMEs, you have manufacturing sector SMEs, you have consulting, you have innovation, you have production. So it has to be a full thing, and we can't forget. The farmers who are the source of the raw material. Cotton plantations, silk weaving places. People who are producing the naturally occurring dyes. Yes, because there's chemical and synthetic dice, but there's naturally occurring dyes. India particularly holds a very big potential because our traditional fabrics. I used to be really fond of my mother's Bandhani saree's from Rajasthan using pulses, and they tie the thread and they make those prints. These are sustainable practices. Yes, but people. Don't appreciate that you need the whole ecosystem to change for there to be decarbonization in this space. Yes, and I also think it's important for SMEs to build consortiums. Like the big industries will always have a, a body like the CII or the FICCI in Karnataka and they have a representation, right. They can go together and say, as a sector, this is what we want. So I think SMEs also should have a kind of a consortium so that their voices are heard and if there is some sector specific decarbonization efforts that they really want to push for, they should be able to voice out their concerns. So are there some avenues like that as well for them? For sure. In fact, you'll be amazed at the level of development that's happening in this space. Organization collective Mobilization has always been around in the Indian ethos, and at least in the post independent India, which has also been historically more socialist than the Western countries. Employ unions have been a thing, and similarly, industry unions have also been a thing. There are trade federations, there are export council federations. Within the apparel sector itself, you have local, regional, multi-regional, national level at all levels. At the moment, however, you do not see a lot of these associations or collectives really focusing on decarbonization. And there's a very simple reason for that. A small manufacturer or an artisan may not have the financial bandwidth to be able to actually invest in the business disruption needed for decarbonization. Right. And even the players in the market are not looking at the ecosystem. In the west it's very easy to say, oh, you need to use sustainable sourcing. Right. It sustainable fashion as an industry, for example very popular in the west, very popular in Delhi and Bangalore and Mumbai. But I look at people in this room and I wonder how many of us have even thought about the clothes being sustainable fashion? It's not possible because we are still price sensitive. And if the customer is price sensitive, the manufacturers are also price sensitive. They cannot afford to take that margin hit because you may produce a sustainable shirt, but then you need 500 rupees 1000 rupees extra. To be able to recoup that cost and the customer is price sensitive, they'll end up going to the local store, which has not sustainably produced material, and then the entire intent is lost. So yes, the sustainable, the decarbonization dimension of it while growing very rapidly has really struggled in India. This sector is a very good example of that because businesses don't yet have the bandwidth and it's unfair to call for, you know, wide sweeping changes without creating an enabling environment for these businesses to transition smoothly without disrupting their cash flows too much. And as you were talking about this, Ishaan, I was thinking about a conference that I attended sometime back, which was hosted by a very well known fashion retail chain on sustainability. And in at that conference they were talking about the traceability of the product and how they have to create awareness in a farming group from where they were sourcing their cotton from Africa. Right. And how, in, in what matrix that does that farmer enter that aspect of whether he's using pesticide in the crop and how that gets configured into the traceability. So there are so many challenges, whether it is sourcing of the material or the dyes used or the amount of water used in the plant. There are any number of challenges in this sector itself. And, and I just couldn't think of another comparison. And that was with regard to dairy farming? Milk production and I happened to be at a hotel in New Zealand, in Southern Island where in, in Queenstown to be specific. And when I was at the hotel, I noticed a lot of milk cans around. So I was wondering why is this hotel having milk cans? And it so happened that there was a conference going on of dairy farmers. Many of them had come from Switzerland and far away regions. And I happened to sit with some of them at breakfast and they were talking about sustainable practices in dairy farming. So I think it's very you know, it's a very unequal world because on one hand they have the resources and they have the funds to travel all the way to a different country, a different continent, and have a discussion about how the sector should be, how it should be heading towards sustainability. While on the other hand, the you know, the small manufacturers are struggling to come to terms with even running a business. In fact, I had a very similar example from the US because, you know, at the grocery stores, they'll have those hydroponically grown lettuce leaves and Bok choy, and then they'll have organic be Trader Joe's. Be Trader Joe's with Walmart stores. With your your, what are the Costcos Yeah. And all of those kinds. And then there'll be a regular stall and I would always go for the, the cheapest blueberries and the cheapest eggs. But there were people who were going for those fancy organic boxes, which were two times, three times the price. In India, if the eggs is 50 rupees yesterday and it's 55 rupees today, I am already complaining. And that is a margin we are not in a space where we can afford that kind of, you know, premium to be paid on sustainability. So it, for us, sustainability cannot just be innovating sustainably. It has also got a affordability dimension and economics dimension. And too dealing the two would be unfair, not just in the Indian context or the global south context anywhere, in the US there are customers that pay that premium, in Europe as well. But in the rest of the world we can't. So we've got to do that calculation to see whether something sustainable is truly going to work or not by looking at its economics. In fact, coffee, we were just having this conversation over coffee and we, we were also talking about how there are so many types of organically grown coffee like from Bolivia Yes. And the South American countries. And coffee is probably the world's second largest traded commodity after oil.
